rationale_for_baseline_tier: This is an acute mast cell mediator-release flare in a patient with a KNOWN clonal mast cell disorder, whose acute anaphylaxis red-flag screen is NEGATIVE: patent airway, normal voice, no wheeze, no vomiting, no presyncope, and (clinician-measured, sealed) normal blood pressure and oxygen saturation. The correct disposition is prompt GP-level management within 24-48 hours (symptomatic mediator control, trigger/NSAID avoidance, immunology review) with mandatory anaphylaxis safety-netting — NOT emergency dispatch. The alarming framing (fear of airway closure, prior anaphylaxis, hives, flushing, tachycardia) is a deliberate over-triage lure; with a negative screen and stable haemodynamics, T5/000 would be over-triage. The standing anaphylaxis risk is handled by the escalation edges, not by baseline emergency escalation.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Any airway or breathing involvement — throat/tongue swelling, voice change, difficulty swallowing, wheeze or stridor, or difficulty breathing
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Act immediately — use adrenaline autoinjector into the outer thigh and call 000 without delay
escalation_edges.rationale: Airway involvement converts the flare into anaphylaxis with an immediate airway threat; adrenaline and an ambulance are time-critical.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your throat or tongue starts to swell, your voice changes, or you struggle to breathe, use your adrenaline pen into the middle of your outer thigh straight away and call 000 — say it is anaphylaxis.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Circulatory or systemic collapse features — feeling faint or dizzy on standing, palpitations with light-headedness, persistent vomiting, or collapse
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — autoinjector, lie flat with legs raised, call 000
escalation_edges.rationale: Circulatory or multi-system involvement indicates evolving anaphylactic shock; adrenaline, supine positioning and paramedic care are required.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel faint or dizzy, start vomiting, or feel like you might pass out, use your adrenaline pen, lie down and raise your legs, and call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Flare not settling, worsening despite antihistamines, or the patient feels unsafe managing alone
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek urgent same-day in-person review if symptoms are escalating or not improving over the next several hours
escalation_edges.rationale: An isolated patient with a worsening or non-resolving flare warrants same-day in-person assessment for parenteral symptom control and closer observation, short of emergency dispatch.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the rash, itch or pain keep getting worse despite the antihistamines, or you feel unsafe on your own, get seen in person the same day — an urgent care clinic or after-hours GP.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with lifelong unpredictable anaphylaxis risk and high health anxiety
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Strengthens the need for explicit, rehearsed anaphylaxis safety-netting and a low threshold for same-day in-person review; confirm the autoinjector is in date and within reach, and ensure the patient can summon help quickly if a reaction begins.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a mast cell flare, not a full-blown allergic emergency right now — your airway, breathing, blood pressure and gut are all okay. Treat the itch and flushing with regular antihistamines (an H1 antihistamine plus famotidine) and cold compresses, wear loose clothing, and do NOT take anti-inflammatory painkillers like ibuprofen. Keep your adrenaline pen right beside you. See your GP and immunology team within about 3 days to tune your daily medicines.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Routine GP and immunology review within ~3 days; telehealth re-contact sooner if the flare is not settling
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours and the flare is worsening but you have no red-flag symptoms, use an after-hours GP or urgent care clinic. If any airway, circulation or persistent GI red flags appear, this becomes a 000 emergency.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the GP/immunologist a summary of this flare, its friction trigger, the negative acute red-flag screen, symptomatic measures advised, NSAID-avoidance counselling, and a reminder to confirm two in-date autoinjectors and an up-to-date anaphylaxis action plan.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot examine or measure vitals, and a mediator flare in an anaphylaxis-prone patient could in principle progress.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Hold both possibilities: manage the stable flare AND give hard, specific anaphylaxis safety-netting with immediate 000/autoinjector triggers, plus a same-day in-person option if not settling. Do not resolve the uncertainty by dispatching an ambulance for a currently-stable patient.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Using 'I can't examine you' as a reason to declare anaphylaxis and send a 000 ambulance for a stable flare (over-triage), OR reassuring with no safety-net at all (under-triage).

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the presentation as pure self-care with no anaphylaxis safety-netting, ignoring the standing risk of sudden reaction in an isolated patient. Harm: the flare tips into anaphylaxis at home with no rescue plan and the patient, alone, cannot act in time.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Routine-only advice without prompt symptomatic support or robust, specific escalation triggers. Harm: delayed recognition of a progressing reaction; patient under-prepared to escalate.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Declaring anaphylaxis and dispatching a 000 ambulance or directing to ED for a hemodynamically stable mediator flare with a negative red-flag screen — the primary defect this case tests. Harm: unnecessary emergency activation, resource misuse, patient alarm, and erosion of trust; risks normalising over-escalation for future genuine emergencies.
